sections.title: Definition of Human Rights
sections.content: Fundamental entitlements every individual possesses against the State or any public authority simply by virtue of belonging to the human family. As outlined in the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR) of 1948: 'rights originating from the inherent dignity of the human person.'

sections.title: Characteristics of Human Rights
rows: 1. Universality
Apply to every individual, regardless of residence, gender, race, religion, culture, or ethnicity
2. Inalienability
Cannot be legitimately stripped from a free individual, nor relinquished or forfeited willingly
3. Indivisibility
Cannot be compartmentalized or withheld; all rights carry equal weight; denying one hinders others
4. Interdependence
The realization of one right is contingent on the achievement of others
5. Inherent
Not bestowed by any individual or governing body; do not need to be acquired or merited
6. Imprescriptible
Do not expire; cannot be forfeited even through non-exercise
7. Essential and Necessary
Without them, it is unfeasible to safeguard moral, physical, social, and spiritual well-being
8. In connection with human dignity
Every person possesses equal worth and dignity; discrimination must not be tolerated
9. Irrevocable
Immutable; cannot be revoked by any entity or authority
10. Participative and Inclusive
Every individual has the right to engage in and access information on processes affecting their well-being
11. Not absolute
Human rights are defined entitlements with specific limitations for the collective welfare
12. Dynamic
Continue to evolve and adapt in response to socio-economic, cultural, and political shifts
13. Limit State Power
States are accountable for adherence to international human rights agreements

sections.title: UDHR — Key Articles
rows: All human beings born free and equal in dignity and rights
Right to life, liberty, and security of person
No slavery or servitude
No torture, cruel, inhuman, or degrading treatment
Right to recognition as a person before the law
Equal protection before the law
No arbitrary arrest, detention, or exile
Presumption of innocence; no retroactive criminal law
Right to privacy
Freedom of movement and residence; right to leave and return to one's country
Right to seek asylum from persecution
Right to a nationality
Right to marry; family as a natural/fundamental group unit
Right to own property
Freedom of thought, conscience, and religion
Freedom of opinion and expression
Freedom of peaceful assembly and association
Right to participate in government; right to equal access to public service
Right to social security
Right to work, free choice of employment, just conditions, equal pay
Right to rest and leisure
Right to adequate standard of living; special care for motherhood and childhood
Right to education; free elementary education
Right to participate in cultural life and benefit from scientific advancement
Everyone has duties to the community; rights subject to limitations determined by law

sections.title: Notable Philippine Legislation on Human Rights
rows: R.A. 11862 (2022) — Expanded Anti-Trafficking in Persons Act
Strengthens mechanisms against trafficking; covers online sexual abuse and child laundering
R.A. 11767 (2022) — Foundling Recognition and Protection Act
Recognizes foundlings as natural-born citizens
Raised minimum age of sexual consent from 12 to 16 years old
R.A. 11642 (2022) — Domestic Administrative Adoption Act
Simplifies domestic adoption; created NACC
Prohibits child marriage; defines it as child abuse
R.A. 11641 (2021) — Department of Migrant Workers Act
Establishes one government agency dedicated to migrant workers
R.A. 11861 (2022) — Expanded Solo Parents Act
Expanded definition of solo parents to include family members of migrant workers abroad for 12+ uninterrupted months
Guarantees protection services for refugees, stateless persons, and asylum seekers
